title: Geodetic coordinates

infobox.value: are a type of [curvilinear](/wiki/Curvilinear_coordinate_system "Curvilinear coordinate system") [orthogonal coordinate system](/wiki/Orthogonal_coordinate_system "Orthogonal coordinate system") used in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") based on a *[reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid")*.

(north/south) ϕ, *[longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude")* (east/west) λ, and **ellipsoidal height** h (also known as **geodetic height**[[1]](#cite_note-National_Geodetic_Survey_(U.S.)._National_Geodetic_Survey_(U.S.)_1986_p._107-1)).

U.S. Department of Commerce, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, National Ocean Service, Charting and Geodetic Services. p. 107. Retrieved 2021-10-24.

Springer Berlin Heidelberg. p. 156. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-642-12124-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-642-12124-1 "Special:BookSources/978-3-642-12124-1"). Retrieved 2021-10-24.

["A guide to coordinate systems in Great Britain"](https://web.archive.org/web/20120211075826/http://www.ordnancesurvey.co.uk/oswebsite/gps/information/coordinatesystemsinfo/guidecontents/). *Ordnance Survey*. Appendices B1, B2. Archived from [the original](http://www.ordnancesurvey.co.uk/oswebsite/gps/information/coordinatesystemsinfo/guidecontents) on 2012-02-11. Retrieved 2012-01-11.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Geographic coordinate system

For broader coverage of this topic, see [Geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Geodetic_coordinates.svg/250px-Geodetic_coordinates.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Geodetic_coordinates.svg)

Geodetic coordinates P(*ɸ*,*λ*,*h*)

**Geodetic coordinates** are a type of [curvilinear](/wiki/Curvilinear_coordinate_system "Curvilinear coordinate system") [orthogonal coordinate system](/wiki/Orthogonal_coordinate_system "Orthogonal coordinate system") used in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") based on a *[reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid")*.
They include **geodetic latitude** (north/south) ϕ, *[longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude")* (east/west) λ, and **ellipsoidal height** h (also known as **geodetic height**[[1]](#cite_note-National_Geodetic_Survey_(U.S.)._National_Geodetic_Survey_(U.S.)_1986_p._107-1)).
The triad is also known as **Earth ellipsoidal coordinates**[[2]](#cite_note-Awange_Grafarend_Paláncz_Zaletnyik_2010_p._156-2) (not to be confused with *[ellipsoidal-harmonic coordinates](/wiki/Ellipsoidal-harmonic_coordinates "Ellipsoidal-harmonic coordinates")*).

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_coordinates&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

Further information: [Longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), [Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), and [Vertical position](/wiki/Vertical_position "Vertical position")

Longitude measures the rotational [angle](/wiki/Angle "Angle") between the zero meridian and the measured point. By convention for the Earth, Moon and Sun, it is expressed in degrees ranging from −180° to +180°. For other bodies a range of 0° to 360° is used.
For this purpose, it is necessary to identify a *zero [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)")*, which for Earth is usually the [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian"). For other bodies a fixed surface feature is usually referenced, which for Mars is the meridian passing through the crater [Airy-0](/wiki/Airy-0 "Airy-0"). It is possible for many different coordinate systems to be defined upon the same reference ellipsoid.

Geodetic latitude measures how close to the poles or equator a point is along a meridian, and is represented as an angle from −90° to +90°, where 0° is the equator. The *geodetic latitude* is the angle between the equatorial plane and a line that is [normal](/wiki/Surface_normal "Surface normal") to the reference ellipsoid. Depending on the flattening, it may be slightly different from the *[geocentric latitude](/wiki/Geocentric_latitude "Geocentric latitude")*, which is the angle between the equatorial plane and a line from the center of the ellipsoid. For non-Earth bodies the terms *[planetographic latitude](/wiki/Planetographic_latitude "Planetographic latitude")* and *[planetocentric latitude](/wiki/Planetocentric_latitude "Planetocentric latitude")* are used instead.

Ellipsoidal height (or ellipsoidal [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")), also known as geodetic height (or geodetic altitude), is the distance between the point of interest and the ellipsoid surface, evaluated along the [ellipsoidal normal vector](/wiki/Ellipsoidal_normal_vector "Ellipsoidal normal vector"); it is defined as a [signed distance](/wiki/Signed_distance "Signed distance") such that points inside the ellipsoid have negative height.

## Geodetic vs. geocentric coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_coordinates&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geodetic vs. geocentric coordinates")]

See also: [Latitude § Geodetic and geocentric latitudes](/wiki/Latitude#Geodetic_and_geocentric_latitudes "Latitude")

Geodetic latitude and *[geocentric latitude](/wiki/Geocentric_latitude "Geocentric latitude")* have different definitions. Geodetic latitude is defined as the angle between the [equatorial](/wiki/Equator "Equator") plane and the [surface normal](/wiki/Surface_normal "Surface normal") at a point on the ellipsoid, whereas geocentric latitude is defined as the angle between the equatorial plane and a radial line connecting the centre of the ellipsoid to a point on the surface (see figure). When used without qualification, the term latitude refers to geodetic latitude. For example, the latitude used in [geographic coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") is geodetic latitude. The standard notation for geodetic latitude is φ. There is no standard notation for geocentric latitude; examples include θ, ψ, φ′.

Similarly, geodetic altitude is defined as the height above the ellipsoid surface, normal to the ellipsoid; whereas *[geocentric altitude](/wiki/Geocentric_altitude "Geocentric altitude")* is defined as the distance to the reference ellipsoid along a radial line to the geocenter. When used without qualification, as in aviation, the term [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude") refers to geodetic altitude (possibly with further refinements, such as in [orthometric heights](/wiki/Orthometric_height "Orthometric height")). Geocentric altitude is typically used in [orbital mechanics](/wiki/Orbital_mechanics "Orbital mechanics") (see [orbital altitude](/wiki/Orbital_altitude "Orbital altitude")).

If the impact of Earth's [equatorial bulge](/wiki/Equatorial_bulge "Equatorial bulge") is not significant for a given application (e.g., [interplanetary spaceflight](/wiki/Interplanetary_spaceflight "Interplanetary spaceflight")), the [Earth ellipsoid](/wiki/Earth_ellipsoid "Earth ellipsoid") may be simplified as a [spherical Earth](/wiki/Spherical_Earth "Spherical Earth"), in which case the geocentric and geodetic latitudes are equal and the latitude-dependent geocentric radius simplifies to a global mean [Earth's radius](/wiki/Earth%27s_radius "Earth's radius") (see also: [spherical coordinate system](/wiki/Spherical_coordinate_system "Spherical coordinate system")).

## Conversion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_coordinates&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Conversion")]

Main article: [Geographic coordinate conversion](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_conversion "Geographic coordinate conversion")

Given geodetic coordinates, one can compute the *[geocentric Cartesian coordinates](/wiki/Geocentric_Cartesian_coordinates "Geocentric Cartesian coordinates")* of the point as follows:[[3]](#cite_note-gps-chap10-3)

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}X&={\big (}N+h{\big )}\cos {\phi }\cos {\lambda }\\Y&={\big (}N+h{\big )}\cos {\phi }\sin {\lambda }\\Z&=\left({\frac {b^{2}}{a^{2}}}N+h\right)\sin {\phi }\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/01ae95fcf2d4b269bdacdabe6fead7e1d9418eea)

where a and b are the equatorial radius ([semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis")) and the polar radius ([semi-minor axis](/wiki/Semi-minor_axis "Semi-minor axis")), respectively. N is the *[prime vertical radius of curvature](/wiki/Prime_vertical_radius_of_curvature "Prime vertical radius of curvature")*, function of latitude ϕ:

:   ![{\displaystyle N={\frac {a^{2}}{\sqrt {a^{2}\cos ^{2}\phi +b^{2}\sin ^{2}\phi }}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/63f12c064536299535ffb7619b78aeccf56ea968)

In contrast, extracting ϕ, λ and h from the rectangular coordinates usually requires [iteration](/wiki/Iterative_method "Iterative method") as ϕ and h are mutually involved through N:[[4]](#cite_note-osgb-4)[[5]](#cite_note-osborne-5)

:   ![{\displaystyle \lambda =\operatorname {atan2} (Y,X)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c4ed0b27d46365b7c4d46c2d6376ad8bff8b58b1).
:   ![{\displaystyle h={\frac {p}{\cos \phi }}-N,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5057954f8b04457c45fb84704353b73fac117079)
:   ![{\displaystyle \phi =\arctan \left((Z/p)/(1-e^{2}N/(N+h))\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5c79e692235c57d3e9f1f805f50310eb0bf9e02e)

where ![{\displaystyle p={\sqrt {X^{2}+Y^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/639768af8b3923c2d304b99aa6c189474de79406). More sophisticated methods are [available](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_conversion#From_ECEF_to_geodetic_coordinates "Geographic coordinate conversion").

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_coordinates&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]
